principles.label: Moral Philosophy Requires Psychology
principles.summary: We must suspend moral philosophy until we develop an adequate philosophy of psychology.
principles.details: Concepts like 'moral obligation' or 'virtue' cannot be properly understood or evaluated without a foundational understanding of what a human action is, how it is affected by motives, and what constitutes intention.
principles.representative_quote: it is not profitable for us at present to do moral philosophy that should be laid aside at any rate until we have an adequate philosophy of psychology in which we are conspicuously lacking

principles.label: Abandon Secular 'Moral Oughts'
principles.summary: The concepts of 'moral obligation' and 'ought' are nonsensical outside of a divine law framework and should be abandoned in secular ethics.
principles.details: These concepts derive their meaning from a law-based conception of ethics (i.e., a divine lawgiver). Without belief in an authoritative legislator, the terms lose their intelligible sense and retain only a harmful, unjustified psychological force.
principles.representative_quote: the concepts of obligation and Duty... ought to be jettisoned if this is psychologically possible because they are survivals or derivatives from survivals from an earlier conception of Ethics which no longer generally survives and are only harmful without it

principles.label: Absolute Prohibition on Murder
principles.summary: Choosing to kill the innocent as a means to an end is always murder, regardless of the consequences.
principles.details: The objective structure of an action matters. Intentionally killing innocent people (such as dropping atomic bombs on civilians) cannot be justified by its ends, nor can it be excused by claiming one was merely directing one's intentions elsewhere.
principles.representative_quote: choosing to kill the innocent as a means to your ends is always murder

principles.label: Action Under a Description
principles.summary: Human actions are only intentional under specific descriptions.
principles.details: A single physical happening can be described in multiple ways. An agent's action (e.g., moving an arm) might be intentional under the description 'pumping water' but not intentional under the description 'contracting these muscles' or 'poisoning the inhabitants'.
principles.representative_quote: an action can be intentional under several descriptions

principles.label: Causality as Derivativeness
principles.summary: Causality consists in the derivativeness of an effect from its causes, not in necessity or universal generalization.
principles.details: An event can derive from a cause without being strictly necessitated by it. We can confidently identify the cause of an event in everyday, individual instances without needing an exceptionless rule that the cause will always produce that effect.
principles.representative_quote: causality consists in the derivativeness of an effect from its causes. This is the core, the common feature of causality in its various kinds.

principles.label: The Non-Referential 'I'
principles.summary: The first-person pronoun 'I' is not a referring expression.
principles.details: If 'I' referred to a physical body, it could be misidentified. If it referred to an immaterial Cartesian ego, we would have no way to identify the same referent across different thoughts over time. Therefore, it is a word with a use but no reference.
principles.representative_quote: And this is the solution: 'I' is neither a name nor another kind of expression whose logical role is to make reference, at all.

principles.label: Practical Truth
principles.summary: Practical truth is brought about and made true by action, rather than by corresponding to an existing reality.
principles.details: Unlike theoretical truth, which is a property of a judgment corresponding to reality, practical truth requires an agent to form a good choice and execute it, making the intentional description of the action true through their doing of it in agreement with right desire.
principles.representative_quote: the truth is brought about made true by action since the description of what one does is made true by his doing it provided that a man forms and executes a good choice

principles.label: Externalism of Mental Content
principles.summary: Brain states are not sufficient conditions for beliefs because mental content is externally determined.
principles.details: The content of a belief depends on the external environment and conceptual context of the subject. A specific brain state cannot constitute a belief about a specific object (like a bank) if the subject's world does not contain that object.
principles.representative_quote: we cannot ascribe a belief about the bank’s opening hours to someone not living in a world of banks and clocks.

principles.label: Error Destroys Action
principles.summary: Ignorance or lack of intent can nullify an action under a certain description.
principles.details: Consent is not a free-floating psychological state; it is directed at an object or action under a specific description. Deception about the nature of that object means consent did not occur for that specific action.
principles.representative_quote: We could enlarge the maxim, in moral philosophy, to 'Error destroys action.'

frameworks.label: The 'Why?' Question of Intention
frameworks.summary: A method for determining if an action, under a specific description, is intentional.
frameworks.details: 1. Observe an event or action. 2. Ask the agent: 'Why are you doing that?' 3. If the agent answers 'I didn't know I was doing that' or 'I merely observed I was doing that', the action is NOT intentional under that description. 4. If the agent answers by giving a reason, mentioning a future end, or simply says 'No reason', the action IS intentional under that description.
frameworks.representative_quote: sincere answers to the why question give reasons that justify and in so justifying explain what one does thereby making the action intelligible to others

frameworks.label: Virtue Ethics Evaluation (Thick Concepts)
frameworks.summary: A framework for evaluating moral actions without relying on divine command or consequentialist calculations.
frameworks.details: 1. Discard the law-like sense of 'ought' and abstract debates about 'moral rightness'. 2. Use philosophy and psychology to determine what things are good for humans as a species. 3. Apply detailed descriptive terms (thick concepts) like 'just', 'unjust', 'courageous', or 'cowardly' to evaluate specific actions. 4. Categorically reject actions that violate these virtues.

frameworks.label: Doctrine of Double Effect
frameworks.summary: A method for evaluating actions that have both good intended effects and bad foreseen side effects.
frameworks.details: 1. Ensure the action itself is not intrinsically forbidden (e.g., murder). 2. Ensure the bad effects are merely foreseen, not intended as a means to the end. 3. Ensure the likely good consequences of the action outweigh its likely bad consequences.
frameworks.representative_quote: I should contend that a man is responsible for the bad consequences of his bad actions but he gets no credit for the good ones and contrarywise is not responsible for the bad consequences of good actions

frameworks.label: Formal vs. Material Object of Action
frameworks.summary: A framework for evaluating the moral culpability of an agent who commits an act in error or ignorance.
frameworks.details: 1. Identify the material object: what actually occurred in the physical world (e.g., shooting a man). 2. Identify the formal object: what the agent believed they were interacting with or doing (e.g., shooting a stag). 3. Evaluate the action and culpability based on the formal object (the agent's understanding), while recognizing the material reality.
frameworks.representative_quote: In one sense—the formal—your object is a stag; in the other—the material—it was a man.

mental_models.label: The Shopping List (Direction of Fit)
mental_models.summary: A metaphor distinguishing between theoretical knowledge (records) and practical knowledge (intentions) based on where the error lies when reality and the document diverge.
mental_models.details: If a detective's record doesn't match what a shopper buys, the mistake is in the record (cognitive state). If the shopper's own list doesn't match what he buys, the mistake is in his performance, not the list (conative state).
mental_models.representative_quote: if the list and the things that the man actually buys do not agree, and if this and this alone constitutes a mistake, then the mistake is not in the list but in the man’s performance

mental_models.label: Brute Facts
mental_models.summary: Facts that make a description true or false, but only relative to a proper institutional context.
mental_models.details: If I loan you money and you promise to pay it back, those are brute facts making it true that you owe me money—but this relies entirely on the unstated institutional context of money and contracts.
mental_models.representative_quote: ...there are certain circumstances where a person’s actions (her example is someone delivering potatoes) can produce the 'brute fact' that someone owes them...

mental_models.label: Intention vs. Side Effects
mental_models.summary: The distinction between what an agent deliberately chooses to do and what they merely foresee will happen as a result of their action.
mental_models.details: This distinction prevents the collapse into consequentialism. An agent is responsible for what falls under the scope of their intention, but they may foresee bad side effects of a good action without intending them.

mental_models.label: The Project Director
mental_models.summary: A metaphor for 'practical knowledge'—the non-observational knowledge an agent has of their own intentional actions.
mental_models.details: A man directing a building project by giving orders knows what is being done without needing to observe it or get reports, just as an agent knows their own intentional actions without needing to observe their own body.
mental_models.representative_quote: class of intentional actions is a sub-class... of the class of things known without observation

mental_models.label: The Solar System's Obligingness
mental_models.summary: A lens for understanding scientific idealization and the limits of determinism.
mental_models.details: The perfect predictability of the solar system under Newtonian mechanics doesn't prove absolute determinism; it merely reflects a system that has luckily avoided external interference (like a rogue planet) during recorded history.
mental_models.representative_quote: it was mere obligingness on the part of the solar system, by having had so peaceful a history in recorded time, to provide such a model

mental_models.label: Truth-Preserving vs. Goodness-Preserving Inference
mental_models.summary: A model distinguishing theoretical reasoning from practical reasoning.
mental_models.details: Theoretical reasoning ensures you never pass from a true premise to a false conclusion. Practical reasoning ensures you never pass from a good end to an action ordered to a bad end.
mental_models.representative_quote: in the sphere of practical reasoning goodness of the end has the same role as truth of the premises has in theoretical reasoning

heuristics.label: Use Thick Moral Concepts
heuristics.summary: Use precise terms of moral evaluation such as 'just' and 'unjust' instead of vague, thin terms like 'morally right' or 'wrong'.

heuristics.label: Suspend Moral Philosophy
heuristics.summary: Stop doing moral philosophy until we have an adequate philosophy of psychology.
heuristics.representative_quote: it is not profitable for us at present to do moral philosophy until we have an adequate philosophy of psychology.

heuristics.label: Never Do Evil That Good May Come
heuristics.summary: A good person will not be tempted by fear or hope of consequences but will do what is choiceworthy come what may.

heuristics.label: Check Definitions of 'Observation'
heuristics.summary: When someone claims you cannot observe a phenomenon (like causality), check if they have defined 'observation' in a way that makes it artificially impossible.

heuristics.label: Action Under Multiple Descriptions
heuristics.summary: Remember that an action can be intentional under one description and not intentional under another.
heuristics.representative_quote: an action can be intentional under several descriptions

signature_quotes.label: Suspend Moral Philosophy
signature_quotes.summary: Anscombe's famous call to halt moral philosophy until psychology is understood.
signature_quotes.details: From her influential paper 'Modern Moral Philosophy'.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: it is not profitable for us at present to do moral philosophy that should be laid aside at any rate until we have an adequate philosophy of psychology in which we are conspicuously lacking

signature_quotes.label: Jettison Moral Obligation
signature_quotes.summary: Her critique of secular moral vocabulary.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: the concepts of moral obligation, the use of the word ought with a special moral sense, and related notions, are harmful and should be dropped

signature_quotes.label: Murder is Always Murder
signature_quotes.summary: Her anti-consequentialist stance on killing innocents.
signature_quotes.details: Protesting Oxford's decision to award Harry Truman an honorary degree.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: choosing to kill the innocent as a means to your ends is always murder

signature_quotes.label: Causality as Derivativeness
signature_quotes.summary: Her definition of causality against Humean necessity.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Causality consists in the derivativeness of an effect from its causes. This is the core, the common feature of causality in its various kinds.

signature_quotes.label: The Word 'I'
signature_quotes.summary: Her rejection of the Cartesian Ego.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The word I is not a name I call myself. It is not a name at all, even though it can appear to be one.

signature_quotes.label: The Shopping List Mistake
signature_quotes.summary: Illustrating the difference between an intention and a record.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: if the list and the things that the man actually buys do not agree, and if this and this alone constitutes a mistake, then the mistake is not in the list but in the man’s performance

signature_quotes.label: Error Destroys Action
signature_quotes.summary: Expanding the legal maxim 'fraud vitiates consent' to broader moral philosophy.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: We could enlarge the maxim, in moral philosophy, to 'Error destroys action.'

signature_quotes.label: All Human Action is Moral
signature_quotes.summary: Equating human action with moral action.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: all Human Action is moral action it is either good or bad it may be both

anti_patterns.label: Consequentialism
anti_patterns.summary: Judging the moral value of an action solely by its consequences.
anti_patterns.details: It collapses the moral distinction between intentionally killing an innocent person and foreseeing their death as a side effect, leading philosophers to justify murder for the greater good. It destroys the concept of justice as a virtue.

anti_patterns.label: Secular 'Moral Oughts'
anti_patterns.summary: Using religious moral concepts (like 'moral obligation') in secular philosophy.
anti_patterns.details: It attempts to enforce a moral law without a law-giver, relying on a leftover conceptual framework from a theistic worldview that modern secular philosophy has otherwise abandoned, rendering the language meaningless.

anti_patterns.label: Equating Causation with Necessitation
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming causality requires a necessary connection or universal generalization.
anti_patterns.details: It forces us to look for an 'iron necessity' or universal generalization that rarely exists. We frequently know what caused an event without needing it to be logically necessary or universally exceptionless.

anti_patterns.label: Treating 'I' as a Referring Expression
anti_patterns.summary: Treating the first-person pronoun 'I' as a referring expression.
anti_patterns.details: It creates a self-perpetuating philosophical dispute that inevitably leads to inventing a Cartesian Ego (an immaterial soul) to serve as the referent, since the physical body cannot fulfill the logical role demanded by this misuse of language.

anti_patterns.label: Misusing 'Direction of Intention'
anti_patterns.summary: Using 'direction of intention' to excuse forbidden acts.
anti_patterns.details: It relies on a false, Cartesian psychology. The objective structure of the action itself constitutes murder, regardless of the private mental gymnastics or 'further intentions' running through the killer's mind.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: The devout Catholic bomber secures by a 'direction of intention' that any shedding of innocent blood that occurs is 'accidental.'

anti_patterns.label: Ignoring Institutional Context
anti_patterns.summary: Treating obligations like 'owing' as mere factual statements independent of their social context.
anti_patterns.details: It ignores that descriptions of actions and transactions require a background of institutional context (brute facts) to be meaningful.
